title: The World Is Full of Married Men
description: A blunt commercial novel about adultery, desire, and male entitlement.
descriptionParagraphs: A blunt commercial novel about adultery, desire, and male entitlement.

The World Is Full of Married Men is usually read through its treatment of sexuality, gender politics, and popular fiction. As a novel, it turns those concerns into conflicts of character, voice, setting, and social pressure rather than leaving them as abstract ideas.

Part of the work's durability lies in the way its form intensifies its themes. Readers return to it not only for subject matter but for the distinctive voice, structure, and atmosphere through which it makes sexuality, gender politics, and popular fiction feel immediate.

cover.alt: The World Is Full of Married Men cover thumbnail from Wikipedia

overview.englishDescription: A blunt commercial novel about adultery, desire, and male entitlement.
banningParagraphs: The World Is Full of Married Men entered censorship debates as a novel associated with sexuality, gender politics, and popular fiction. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around obscenity and morality.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1968 in Australia, where Australian censors banned importation. Officials deemed the book too sexually explicit for circulation. It is part of a larger pattern in which mass-market women's commercial fiction drew heavy state scrutiny.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: Literature Suppressed on Sexual Grounds

counterReadings.note: Surveys the legal and moral language used to suppress books as obscene.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.
